coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are becoming a desirable alternative to metal-based QDs due to their high biocompatibility, low toxicity, ease of preparation, and unique photophysical properties. In the present study, a simple microwave-assisted synthesis using a domestic oven has been adopted to produce multifunctional CQDs from the ethlyenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and 4, 7, 10-trioxa-1, 13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) monomers. The molecular surface of the prepared CQDs is composed of hyperbranched polyamides obtained from the polymerization reaction of both EDTA and TTDDA. The controlled functionalities and chemical structures of the molecular surface of CQDs are attained by varying the initial feed ratio of EDTA and TTDDA from 1:1 to 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, 1:5, and 3:1. The T-richCD3 (EDTA: TTDDA = 1:4) with highest mass yield of 25 wt% exhibited five-folds higher photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) (53.3%) compared to E-richCD1 (EDTA: TTDDA = 3:1). All synthesized CQDs revealed good sensitivity and selectivity to a Cu2+ ion that is prone to cause different neurodegenerative diseases and obstruct many biological activities, while the sensitivity increased with the decrease in EDTA to TTDDA ratio. The zebrafish model has been used to demonstrate the toxicity, adverse effects, transport, and biocompatibility of CQDs. At higher concentrations (1.0 mg/mL), the T-richCDs exhibited superior cell viability than the E-richCDs for Madin-Darby Canine Kidney (MDCK) and human breast cancer MDA-MB-231 (MDAMB) cells. Because of the excellent down conversion fluorescence ability, T-richCD3 were employed as luminescent down-shifting (LDS) layer in CuInSe2 solar cell to enhance its performance.

The inset of (b) represents the EQE of the T-richCD3/PMMA-coated device normalized with respect to that of the bare device.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are becoming a desirable alternative to metal-based QDs due to their high biocompatibility, low toxicity, ease of preparation, and unique photophysical properties. In the present study, a simple microwave-assisted synthesis using a domestic oven has been adopted to produce multifunctional CQDs from the ethlyenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and 4, 7, 10-trioxa-1, 13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) monomers. The molecular surface of the prepared CQDs is composed of hyperbranched polyamides obtained from the polymerization reaction of both EDTA and TTDDA. The controlled functionalities and chemical structures of the molecular surface of CQDs are attained by varying the initial feed ratio of EDTA and TTDDA from 1:1 to 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, 1:5, and 3:1. The T-richCD3 (EDTA: TTDDA = 1:4) with highest mass yield of 25 wt% exhibited five-folds higher photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) (53.3%) compared to E-richCD1 (EDTA: TTDDA = 3:1). All synthesized CQDs revealed good sensitivity and selectivity to a Cu2+ ion that is prone to cause different neurodegenerative diseases and obstruct many biological activities, while the sensitivity increased with the decrease in EDTA to TTDDA ratio. The zebrafish model has been used to demonstrate the toxicity, adverse effects, transport, and biocompatibility of CQDs. At higher concentrations (1.0 mg/mL), the T-richCDs exhibited superior cell viability than the E-richCDs for Madin-Darby Canine Kidney (MDCK) and human breast cancer MDA-MB-231 (MDAMB) cells. Because of the excellent down conversion fluorescence ability, T-richCD3 were employed as luminescent down-shifting (LDS) layer in CuInSe2 solar cell to enhance its performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a generic term for a variety of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials including carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and polymer dots (PDs), have triggered extensive studies because of their fascinating properties such as high water solubility, good physiological compatibility, low toxicity, robust chemical inertness, tunable fluorescence emission colors, superior photochemical stability, and facile surface functionalization[1–4]. These features of CDs offer tremendous potential in an array of applications including biomedicine, optoelectronics, catalysis, and sensing [5–9].

Both top-down and bottom-up approaches have been intensively exploited to synthesize large quantities of CDs with high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) using environmental-friendly precursor materials [10]. “Top-down” synthetic approaches, i.e., the break-down of bulk carbon-rich precursor materials such as graphite [11], carbon nanotubes [12], activated carbon [13], and nanodiamonds [14] using electrochemical techniques [15], laser ablation, [16] and others, have routinely produced single-layered GQDs applicable to various optoelectronics including light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and solar cells [6,17]. On the other hand, various small molecules and polymers have been dehydrated and further carbonized through various bottom-up approaches including hydro/solvothermal treatment and pyrolysis/combustion to produce CQDs, CNDs, and PDs [1,18–20].

In previous research, the main focus has been on finding the environmental-friendly resources for the synthesis of CDs, [10] precise control of the apparent optical band-gap energy of CDs by tuning the size or surface oxidation level, [21,22] maximization of the PLQY through surface passivation or doping with hetero-atoms, [23,24] and precise exploitation of the fluorescence behavior and operation mechanism of the CDs [25]. While it is clear that the surface molecular structures of CDs exert tremendous effects on the chemical, physical, and optoelectronic properties of CDs, little attention has been paid to control the molecular surface of CDs precisely. Indeed, the surface functionalities of CDs are not easily tunable due to the use of harsh reaction conditions (high reaction temperature, prolonged reaction period, or even strong acidic medium), where the facile oxidation of the molecular surface of CDs often endows their surface with high levels of carboxylic acid residues.

Very recently, intensive research interest has been focused on the synthesis of CQDs from polyamidation monomer sets using the bottom-up approaches, especially through microwave-assisted synthesis (MAS). The fast reaction time (routinely<10 min), high mass yields (often more than 50 wt%), cheap MAS operation (enabling the use of household microwave ovens), medium to high PLQYs (ranging between 10% and 49%), very low cytotoxicity of MAS-mediated CQDs (regardless of the natural abundance of the starting materials), and facile compositional control of the prepared CQDs (through variation of the feed ratio of multiple monomers) have been exploited to synthesize multifunctional CQDs for various applications including cellular fluorescence imaging, fluorescent polymer composites, and down-converting layers on Si solar cells [1–7]. So far, AB2 (A: COOH, B: NH2), A2/B3, and A3/B2 type polyamidation monomer sets have been successfully examined for the synthesis of self-passivated and multifunctional CQDs with improved optical performance (such as high PLQYs through MAS), which demonstrates the importance of “branching point(s)” in the starting materials, as the simultaneous dehydration, condensation, and carbonization processes that are essential for the synthesis of CQDs may be accelerated with the branched or hyperbranched morphologies of polycondensates relative to that of the linear counterparts [26–28].

However, detailed structural investigation of the aforementioned MAS-mediated dehydration, condensation, and carbonization processes for the branched polyamidation monomer sets has rarely been attempted. Although MAS-mediated CQDs have clear carbogenic graphitic lattice structures in their cores, the tuning and detailed evaluation of the surface chemistry of the molecular surfaces of MAS-mediated CQDs has never been attempted before. So far, it has only been shown that rich amine or amide functionalities exist on the molecular surface of MAS-mediated CQDs prepared from polyamidation monomer sets, which result in the formation of self-passivated CQDs with high PLQYs. Therefore, an in-depth investigation of the chemical structure of the molecular surface of MAS-mediated CQDs is urgently required for achieving precise on-demand control of the surface chemical structure of MAS-mediated CQDs, which is especially advantageous for the design of CQDs with desired optoelectronic properties.

In this study, ethlyenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and 4, 7, 10-trioxa-1, 13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) have been used as A4 and B2 type polyamidation monomer sets, respectively. EDTA is a well-known chelating ligand with high binding affinity for several heavy metal cations such as Cu2+, and the synthesis of MAS-mediated CQDs is accelerated with the use of EDTA because of its branched molecular structure. TTDDA has an oligo-ethylene glycol backbone and provides amphiphilic solubility for the resulting CQDs. Precise structural evaluation of MAS-mediated CQDs synthesized with different initial feed ratios of EDTA and TTDDA reveal that the molecular surface of MAS-based CQDs is thoroughly consistent with the chemical structures of poly-condensates obtained from both EDTA and TTDDA. The toxicity, biocompatibility, and Cu2+ ion sensing ability of these CQDs have been investigated. Further, CQDs exhibiting high PLQY are employed as a luminescent downshifting layer to enhance the performance of CuInSe2 solar cell. While these findings are meaningful for understanding the molecular surface and possible application of MAS-mediated CQDs, it may also be very important for the construction of other functional CQDs because certain innate optoelectronic, catalytic, physiological, and electrochemical functions of polyamidation monomer sets can be largely preserved in the foams of branched polyamides on the molecular surface of the resulting MAS-mediated CQDs.

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, 98.5%) was purchased from Daejung Chemical Corp. (South Korea). The 4, 7, 10-trioxa-1, 13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA, 97%) and poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA, average molecular weight of up to 120,000 Da) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). All organic solvents including N, N-dimethlyformamide (DMF, 99.5%), ethanol (99.5%), and chloroform (99.5%) were purchased from Samchun Chemical Corp. (South Korea). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. For carrying out dialysis, molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) membranes (3,500 Da) of Spectrum Laboratories, Inc. (USA) were used. A domestic microwave oven (700 W) of Daewoo Electronics Corp. (South Korea) was utilized throughout the experiments.

MAS-based CQDs were synthesized by tuning the molar equivalent ratios of EDTA and TTDDA. Briefly, 3.0 g of EDTA was separately reacted with 4.6, 6.8, 9.3, and 11.4 mL of TTDDA to tailor the EDTA to TTDDA molar ratio from 1:2 to 1:3, 1:4 and 1:5, and the obtained CQDs were named as T-richCD1, T-richCD2, T-richCD3, and T-richCD4, respectively. In an alternative set of experiments, 2.3 mL (10 mmol) of TTDDA was separately reacted with 9.0 and 3.0 g of EDTA to attain the EDTA to TTDDA molar ratios of 3:1 and 1:1, and the obtained CQDs were named as E-richCD1 and E-richCD2 (Table S1).

In detail, predetermined amounts of EDTA and TTDDA were completely dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and the resultant precursor solution was transferred into a 250 mL high temperature-resistant Erlenmeyer flask. The mouth of the flask was loosely covered with polyethylene wrapping films and the flask was placed inside the microwave oven to expose the precursor solution to microwave heating for 5 min. Caution! As volatile toxic gaseous materials can be released from the reaction mixture, microwave heating should be operated inside of the fume hood! Also, pay extreme attention while handling the flask because the reaction temperature is very high just after the microwave operation. After the microwave operation, the dark-brown solid residues were cooled down to room temperature naturally and then dissolved into 100 mL of deionized water to form a crude dark brown CQD solution. Any insoluble contents were removed from the CQD solution by centrifugation at 6,000 rpm for 60 min. Then, the crude CQD solution was purified inside the dialysis membrane for 3 days to remove any non-reacted small molecules and ionic impurities. Finally, the dialyzed CQD solutions were freeze-dried under vacuum to yield brown solids of purified CQDs. The maximum mass yield (based on the initial total mass of EDTA and TTDDA) of 25 wt% was achievable in the synthesis of T-rich CQD3.

The relative QY (ϕ) of prepared CQDs was calculated from equation (1), known as the single-point (single-concentration) method [29,30]:

here, I, A, and n indicate the fluorescence emission intensity, the refractive index, and the optical density, respectively. The subscript R refers to quinine sulfate and the QY (ϕR) of quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as a standard (ϕR = 54% at 360 nm).

The experiments of metal cation detection were carried out by previously reported procedure [31]. To demonstrate the high sensitivity and selectivity of the CQDs for heavy metal ion detection, Cd2+ (CdCl2), Cr3+ (Cr(NO3)3), Cu2+ (CuSO4·5H2O), Fe2+ (Fe(NH4)2(SO4)2), K+ (KCl), Mg2+ (MgCl2), Na+ (NaNO3), Pb2+ (PbCl2), and Zn2+ (ZnCl2) ions were selected. These metal cation solutions (1.5 mL, 50 μM) and CQD solutions (3.5 mL, 0.02 mg/mL) were mixed together and then the PL spectra were recorded.

A cell viability test was carried out using MTT (3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) reagent. Briefly, 200 μL of each MDCK (Madin-Darby Canine Kidney) mammalian cell lines and MDA-MB-231 (human breast cancer) cell lines in RPMI medium (105 cells/mL) was transferred into a 96-well plate at 37 ℃ as the physiological temperature with 5% CO2 for one day to incubate the cell cultures. For the evaluation of cellular viability, the cells were treated with EDTA, TTDDA, and CQDs up to the concentration of 1 mg/mL (1000 rpm) in RPMI medium and then incubated for a day.

The zebrafish (Danio rerio, wild type) used in the study were approximately 7–8 months old and were bred at 28 ± 1 ℃ with a 14/10 h light/dark cycle in a carbon-filtered water system. The breeding methods were performed according to the zebrafish book [32]. The zebrafish were fed brine shrimp (Artemia cysts, INVE Aquaculture, Inc., Belgium) three times per day. The zebrafish were mated at the male and female ratio of 2:1 and the collected embryo was used for the experiment. The protocol of Kimmel et al. was followed for embryonic developmental stages [33]. All experiments were conducted under the supervision and approval of the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee at Kyung Hee University, South Korea.

Embryos were randomly divided into the following groups: Control, EDTA, TTDDA, E-richCD1, T-richCD1, and T-richCD4 exposed groups. Each experimental sample was prepared at dilute concentrations (100 mg/L) after ultrasonic dispersion of the material for 5 min. Embryos (n=120 per group) placed in 6-well plate (20 embryos and 10 mL diluted sample solution per well) and incubated at 28 ± 10 ℃ for 72 h. Embryos were observed at 4, 12, 24, 32, 48, 52, and 72 h post-fertilization with the microscope (Olympus, SZ61, Japan). These time points were selected because they correspond to known developmental stages of a particular part such as the eye, notochord, and otolith [33]. The hatching and abnormal rate (%) of the larvae were observed after removing dead embryos during the incubation for 72 h throughout the experiment to avoid contamination.
Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was conducted using R version 3.0.2 (Frisbee Sailing) and post-hoc tests utilized Tukey’s honestly significant difference (HSD) test. P values were calculated P < 0.01.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CuInSe2 (CISe) solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: The CuInSe2 (CISe) thin films were fabricated on Mo-coated soda-lime glass (SLG) substrates by an electrodeposition technique. A detailed description of the CISe deposition process can be found elsewhere [34]. The as-deposited CISe films were initially pre-annealed at 300 °C for 30 min and subsequently annealed at 580 °C for 30 min in a Se vapor atmosphere to improve the crystallinity and microstructure of the films. For the device fabrication, the annealed CISe films were chemically etched in an aqueous solution containing 0.1 M KCN for 1 min. The solar cell devices were completed by the sequential deposition of a CdS buffer layer (~60 nm) via chemical bath deposition and a window layer composed of ZnO (~50 nm) and Al2O3-doped ZnO (AZO, ~450 nm) via radio-frequency (RF) magnetron sputtering. Ni/Al top contacts were deposited by thermal evaporation. Therefore, the final configuration of the solar cell devices is represented as SLG/Mo/CISe/CdS/ZnO/AZO/Ni/Al.

To investigate the effect of the carbon quantum dot/poly (methyl methacrylate) (CQD/PMMA) composites on the photovoltaic (PV) performance of the CISe solar cells, a T-richCD3 (0.36 wt%)/PMMA (99.7 wt%) composite solution dissolved in chloroform (0.5 g/mL) was spin-coated at 3000 rpm for 60 s on top of the completed devices and subsequently dried for 24 h at ambient temperature. To assess the optical effect of PMMA matrix of the composites, CISe devices coated with only PMMA were also investigated.

The optical absorption spectra of CQD solutions were measured by Optizen Alpha UV–Vis Smart Spectrometer of Mecasys (South Korea). PL spectra of CQD solutions were obtained from the Fluoro Mate FS-2 PL spectrometer of Scinco Corp. (South Korea). Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR) spectra were measured from Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and both 1H and 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were measured from Avance 400FT NMR spectrometer (400 MHz, Bruker) in deuterium oxide (D2O). The surface chemical compositions of the CQDs were probed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Scientific Sigma Probe Multi-2000 spectrometer (Thermo VG Scientific Co., USA). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) analysis was performed using Tecnai F30 ST Field TEM (FEI Corporation) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) studies were carried out using Multimode-N3-AM nanoscope three-dimensional (3D) scanning probe microscopy (SPM) system (Bruker Corp.). The surface reflectance of the devices was characterized by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 35, Perkin Elmer, Inc.) using a fixed-angle specular reflectance attachment. The current density–voltage (j–V) characteristics were measured under AM 1.5G illumination with a light intensity of 1 sun (100 mW/cm2) using a solar simulator (Yamashita Denso, YSS-50S). The external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra were obtained with an incident photon to current efficiency system (PV Measurements, Inc.) using a 75 W xenon light source (Ushio Inc.). Before measurement, calibration was carried out using NIST-calibrated silicon and germanium photodiodes in the spectral ranges of 300 to 1100 nm and 1100 to 1400 nm, respectively.

CQDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted condensation and carbonization of EDTA and TTDDA as A4 and B2 type polyamidation monomers (A: COOH, B: NH2), respectively. Competitive amidation or carbonization of the three-dimensional morphology with branching points in the presence of EDTA molecules produced strongly fluorescent and water-dispersible CQDs within 5 min of microwave treatment using a domestic microwave oven. By varying the molar feed ratio of EDTA to TTDDA (3:1 to 1:5), a total six types of CQDs were prepared. CQDs prepared with EDTA to TTDDA molar ratio of 3:1 and 1:1 (NCOOH:NNH2 = 6:1 and 2:1) are denoted as E-richCD1 and E-richCD2, respectively, while T-richCD1, T-richCD2, T-richCD3, and T-richCD4 represents CQDs prepared from EDTA and TTDDA with molar ratio of 1:2 to 1:3, 1:4 and 1:5 (NCOOH:NNH2 = 1:1, 1:1.5, 1:2, and 1:2.5), respectively (Table S1).

The surface morphology of CQDs was investigated by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. 1 ). The HR-TEM images of E-richCD1 and T-richCD4 reveal the presence of semi-spherical particles with lateral dimensions well below 20 nm (Fig. 1a, c). Further, the crystalline carbogenic lattice is absent in the core area, but present in the shell area of the CQDs. The graphitic lattice structures in the outer shell area of the CQDs were identified in the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern showed in Fig. 1(b, d) of the CQDs. The observed lattice spacing of 0.21 nm for E-richCD1 (Fig. 1a, b), exactly matches with the lattice constant along the x-y axis of the graphene plane i.e., corresponds to the (100) plane, and T-richCD4 showed a lattice spacing distance of 0.34 nm (Fig. 1c, d), which is consistent with the interplanar distance (z-axis) of the graphite crystal structure. The AFM image of E-richCD2 shows dot-like morphologies with thickness in the range of 11–16 nm, in accordance with the TEM data depicted in Fig. 1(a, c). The origin of the unique morphological feature of the CQDs is not quite clear at this stage, but generally MAS-mediated condensation and carbonization of polyamidation monomers produce CQDs with various morphologies such as spherical, semi-spherical, or onion-like, etc. The formation of a specific morphology depends on the specific chemical reactivity and structure of the starting polyamidation monomers. All prepared CQDs are soluble in polar solvents (water, ethanol, and methanol) and even in less polar solvents (tetrahydrofuran and chloroform), which might be due to the presence of polar functional groups such as hydroxyls, amines, and carboxylic acids on the surface of the CQDs, as well as the incorporation of amphiphilic oligo-ethylene glycol segments of TTDDA throughout the CQDs (Fig. S1) [26]. Further, the drastic decrease in PL intensity observed in less polar solvents could result from the partial solubility of CQDs in these solvents (Fig. S1). This amphiphilic solubility of the CQDs may be highly beneficial for their use in various applications, specifically in optoelectronics because the direct exposure of most optoelectronic devices to water can be harmful.

The detailed chemical composition and molecular structure of CQDs were investigated using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ). The characteristic amide I and II peaks at 1654 and 1547 cm−1 were observed in the FTIR spectra of all CQDs [28]. It confirms that the main condensation reaction during MAS-mediated synthesis of CQDs corresponds to the amidation reaction between the amine groups of TTDDA and carboxylic acid groups of EDTA [28,35]. Further, interestingly, the C=O stretching peak of the carboxylic acid groups (ν(C=O)) was observed at 1702 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of all CQDs, implying that the EDTA-rich CQDs possess significant amounts of unreacted -COOH groups on their surface, despite the high TTDDA feed ratio [36].

The XPS survey spectra of E-richCD1 and T-richCD4 show similar patterns comprising three major constituent peaks of C1s, N1s, and O1s as depicted in Fig. S2 (a, b). The detailed elemental composition of E-richCD1 and T-richCD4 is tabulated in Table S2. Fig. 3 compares the high-resolution C1s, O1s, and N1s XPS spectra corresponding to the E-richCD1 and T-richCD4, respectively. From the peak fitting analysis, the C1s peak of T-richCD4 is mainly contributed by C-N/C-O (285.8 eV) species (46.3%), while second major (42.1%) and least (11.6%) contributions are from C-C/C=C (284.5 eV) and C=O (287.5 eV) groups, respectively [26]. In the case of E-richCD1, the relative contribution of C-C/C=C decreased to 31.8% while the intensity of the C=O peak increased (22.9%) and shifted towards higher binding energy, indicating the rise in the surface oxidized moieties percentage. Consequently, the O1s region of both E-richCD1 and T-richCD4 displayed contributions from oxygen bonded to carbon with C=O (532 eV), C-O (531 eV) and O-C=O (~533.5 eV) configurations [26], where the fraction of oxygen atoms bonded to C through C-O is higher in the case of E-richCD1. Further, the high-resolution N1s spectra reveal the existence of pyrrolic, pyridinic, and graphitic N atoms within the graphitic hexagon rings of E-richCD1 and T-richCD4 (Fig. 3c) [37].

Detailed information on the molecular structures of CQDs was obtained via 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra for E-richCD1 and T-richCD4 (Figs. 4 and S3). Interestingly, E-richCD1 and T-richCD4 showed different proton peak patterns, with chemical shifts in the 4.5–2.5 ppm range. The 1H NMR spectrum of E-richCD1 showed the presence of protons, corresponding to EDTA-terminated molecular structures. The proton between two neighboring amines of EDTA produced signals at 2.88 ppm, and the protons between the amine and carboxylic acid (or amide) produced peaks at 3.99, 3.79, 3.71, and 3.36 ppm. In comparison, the 1H NMR spectrum of T-richCD4 is significantly different. The proton signals of the EDTA-moieties were shifted to 2.58 and 3.27 ppm, showing that most of the carboxylic acid groups of EDTA are amidated upon high TTDDA inclusion (i.e., for the T-richCDs). In addition, the protons of the TTDDA moieties presented signals at 2.76 and 3.15 ppm, showing that T-richCD4 contained numerous terminal amine functionalities originating from mono-substituted TTDDA. Overall, the 1H NMR analysis supports that by judicious variation of the initial feed ratio, the dominant functionalities in the as-formed CQDs can be controlled. 13C NMR analysis again demonstrated the different molecular surfaces of the E-richCD1 and T-richCD4 (Fig. S3). The 13C NMR spectrum of E-richCD1 showed the presence of multiple carbonyl peaks at 172, 170, and 165 ppm, originating from both amide and carboxylic acid groups. In comparison, the profile of T-richCD4 showed a single carbonyl peak at 173 ppm, which reveals that amide linkages are dominant on the molecular surface of the T-richCDs. Notably, the NMR analysis demonstrated that the chemical structure of the molecular surface of the CQDs prepared from both EDTA and TTDDA closely matches the chemical structure of the condensates with hyperbranched moieties obtained from EDTA and TTDDA. Therefore, it is proposed that the molecular surface of the CQDs is very similar to that of the hyperbranched condensates obtained from the general polymerization reaction of EDTA and TTDDA.

For the comparison of absorption and emission properties of CQDs in an aqueous solution (0.1 g/L), the prepared-CQDs (E-richCD1 and T-richCD3) were investigated by ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectroscopies (Fig. 5 ). The UV–vis spectra of E-richCD1 and T-richCD3 revealed strong absorption peaks at 237 nm and 220 nm, respectively, which can be attributed to the π - π * transition of aromatic sp2 carbons in multiple-aromatic chromophores ( π -conjugated carbon framework) [38]. A weak broad absorption peak between 300 and 370 nm observed for both CQDs is due to the n- π * transition of the carbonyl group (C=O moieties) [38]. Interestingly, the absorption intensities of the π - π * and n- π * transitions of CQDs decreased with an increase in the TTDDA equivalent.

As shown in Fig. 5b and c, E-richCD1 and T-richCD3 displayed a maximum emission intensity at 475.4 nm (blue) and 430.8 nm (blue), respectively, upon excitation with 280 nm UV light. Also, these CQDs exhibited an excitation wavelength-dependent emission similar to the previously reported CQDs synthesized by bottom-up approaches [39]. The obtained quantum yield (QY) of the T-richCD3 is 53.3%, the quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 is used as a reference. The QY of the T-richCD3 (53.3%) is almost five folds higher than the QY of the E-richCD1 (11.4%), which can be attributed to the formation of favorable emissive states due to the presence of nitrogen-rich groups in the former CQDs [40].

To demonstrate the high sensitivity and selectivity of all CQDs towards heavy metal ion detection, several metal ions including Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+, and Zn2+ are selected for the analysis and results are depicted in Fig. 6 . These metal ions showed high toxicity and natural abundance in the environment, commonly found in foods, medicines, and living organisms, etc. Generally, the emission wavelength and intensity of CQDs can be altered by adding metal ions due to the photoinduced charge transfer processes [41]. Therefore, CQDs can work as good metal detecting sensors with high sensitivity and better selectivity towards heavy metal ions. Fig. 6 concludes that the Cu2+ ions decreased the relative fluorescence intensities of all CQDs, with a percentage of quenching more than 30% in the case of the T-richCD4. Further, a somewhat less significant quenching effect is also demonstrated by Fe2+ and Zn2+ ions in the case of E-richCD1 and T-richCD4, respectively (Fig. 6). The amide and carboxylic acid functional groups present on the surface of CQDs facilitates the interaction between CQDs and metal ions [9,42]. CQDs' higher sensitivity towards Cu2+ ions compared to other metal ions is due to the higher thermodynamic affinity and chelating ability of Cu2+ ions with the N and O of the CQDs.Therefore, the interaction of Cu2+ ions with CQDs surface functional groups result in CQD-Cu2+ complexes. Under the photoexcitation, electron from CQDs transferred to the positively charged Cu2+ ion and causes the photoluminescence quenching [9,42]. The direct interaction of Cu2+ ions with CQDs without the requirement of any post-synthesis surface modification/doping is very beneficial for potential large-scale production and applications (Table S3).

The outstanding fluorescence and complete water solubility of all CQDs prompted to investigate their application as biocompatible and non-toxic fluorescent bio-imaging agents. So, MTT-mediated cell viability assay is conducted with Madin Darby canine kidney (MDCK) epithelial cells and MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells. The MTT assay revealed high cell viability incorporation with all prepared-CQDs (E-richCD1, T-richCD1, and T-richCD4) and the precursors (EDTA and TTDDA) (Figs. 7 and S4). In the case of EDTA, the cell viability is decreased up to 89.7% with an increase in the concentration up to 1.0 mg/mL (Fig. S4). Promisingly, at the high concentration (1.0 mg/mL), the obtained cell viabilities are (95.9%, 95.6%) for E-richCD1, (96.7%, 97.4%) for T-richCD1, and (96.6%, 97.2%) for T-richCD4 in the case of (MDCK, MDAMB) cells. Overall, all T-richCDs are more biocompatible and nontoxic candidates as compared to E-richCDs, for anticancer drugs delivery system (Figs. 7 and S4). Further, these CQDs have shown comparable or better biocompatibility than those reported for fluorescent carbon-based QDs (Table S4).

Zebrafish (Danio rerio) is a prominent animal model that has been applied extensively in the medical and chemical research to study the toxicity and biocompatibility of nanomaterials and drugs [43,44]. Compared to other vertebrate model systems such as mice, rats, and human, zebrafish offers attractive features such as highly transparent embryo, easy maintenance, and rapid embryonic development [43,44]. Especially, the transparency of zebrafish embryo allows a unique opportunity to directly observe the nanoparticles/drugs induced abnormalities or malformations of internal organs. To determine the effect of precursors (EDTA and TTDDA) and as-prepared CQDs (E-richCD1, T-richCD1, and T-richCD4) on embryonic development, we treated embryos with 100 mg/L concentration of respective chemical or CQDs before recording the developmental indices such as hatching rate and abnormal rate at 72 h post-fertilization. The results in Figs. 8 and S5 show that both hatching rate and abnormal rate of treated zebrafish are highly dependent on the type of CQDs or precursor chemicals. At 72 h, 89.47% of control embryos are hatched, while the hatching rate of groups exposed to E-richCD1, T-richCD1, and T-richCD4 are 66.67 ± 2.89%, 74.26 ± 3.94%, and 70 ± 5%, respectively. Although the hatching rate of CQD exposed groups decreased compared to that of the control, the decline is not significant when compared to zebrafish treated with GO, ZnO, CdSe, Ag, CuO, and NiO nanoparticles [45–47]. Meanwhile, the most noticeable result is that the abnormal rate of the group exposed to EDTA is less than the control group, and the groups exposed to CQDs with the high initial feed ratio of EDTA (E-rich CD1 and T-richCD1) don’t have the abnormal rate at all. On the other hand, even though the group exposed to TTDDA shows a higher abnormal rate than the control group, the abnormal rate of T-richCD4 group remarkably decreased. According to the result, it is undeniable that zebrafish exposed to CQDs environment have the possibilities of being born normal are much higher than when exposed to EDTA, TTDDA, and even control. Fig. S5 shows the specific abnormal morphologies among the total surviving individuals (%) in the control and the groups exposed to EDTA, TTDDA, and T-richCD4. Heart and yolk defects are observed in a number of abnormal individuals in the control group. The EDTA exposed group had lower levels of heart and yolk defects compared to the control group. The groups exposed to TTDDA and T-richCD4 had high levels of heart and yolk defects compared to the control group (Fig. S6). Thus, TTDDA partially contributes to heart and yolk defects during the development of zebrafish embryo. Table S5 lists toxic responses observed in various QDs and nanoparticles treated zebrafish. Overall, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of CQDs synthesized using a comparatively higher proportion of EDTA in the precursor (E-richCD1 and T-richCD1) clearly reveal a great potential in bioimaging.

In silicon solar cells and thin-film solar cells of various kinds, the typical low external quantum efficiency (EQE) in the shorter wavelength regime of the solar spectrum leaves much room for the improvement of overall power conversion efficiency (PCE) [48,49]. The Cu(In1–xGax)Se2 (CIGS) thin-film solar cell suffers from this issue, because the high energy photons in the UV–blue wavelength regime of the incident solar irradiation are absorbed by buffer (CdS, Eg = 2.4 eV) and window (ZnO, Eg = 3.4 eV) layer before reaching the absorber [50]. However, the carriers generated in the CdS and ZnO layers are vulnerable to recombination due to charge carrier depletion anisotropy near the heterojunction, thereby leading EQE loss at shorter wavelengths [50]. The use of a suitable luminescent down-shifting (LDS) layer in the solar cell has proven to enhance the spectral response in the shorter wavelength region [51]. Herein, in an attempt to address this issue, a luminescent down-shifting (LDS or down-conversion) layer is composed of T-richCD3/PMMA, which absorbs high energy photons and reemits photons with longer wavelengths, was utilized in CuInSe2 (CISe) solar cells. Fig. 9 (a, b) depicted the current–voltage (j–V) and EQE characteristics of the CISe devices (Fig. S7) measured before and after coating the T-richCD3/PMMA composite onto the aluminum-doped zinc oxide (AZO) layer to explore the LDS effect of the CQDs. After the LDS layer coating, the device exhibited a significant enhancement (from 38.1 to 39.9 mA/cm2) in the short-circuit current density (j
SC ) (i.e., 4.7% enhancement). From EQE curve, an improved spectral response is observed at the shorter-wavelength region in the presence of LDS layer. This can be seen more clearly in the normalized EQE curve (inset Fig. 9b) of the T-richCD3/PMMA-coated device with respect to that of the bare device.

Neglecting the large scatter in the value of EQECQDs/EQEBare at 350 nm, about 9% enhancement in EQE is observed due to LDS of T-richCD3/PMMA layer for CISe solar cell. The photocurrent efficiency (PCE) of the T-richCD3/PMMA-coated CISe solar cell is also calculated from the JV curves (Fig. 9a) and found to be higher (11.1%) than that of the bare solar cell (10.8%). The PCE enhancement achieved with T-richCD3/PMMA was not as great as jSC enhancement. Unfortunately, the fill factor (FF) of the CISe solar cell declined from 0.651 to 0.632 (by 2.9%) due to T-richCD3/PMMA coating. As per the literature, the decrease in FF is mainly due to an increase in the series resistance [52].

It should be noted that here the PMMA in the composite layer may serve as an anti-reflection (AR) medium, [53,54] and hence the optical gain due to PMMA could be included in the photocurrent enhancement of the T-richCD3/PMMA-coated device observed in Fig. 9a and b [55]. In order to deconvolute the LDS contribution of the CQDs from the AR effect of PMMA, a CISe device coated with PMMA only is also fabricated and its PV performance is compared with that of the bare device. As shown in Fig. 9c, the PCE of the PMMA-coated CISe solar cell found to be 9.8% which is lower than that of bare solar cell (10.4%). The results also show a decreased FF from 0.618 to 0.605. Thus, in PMMA coated CISe solar cell, the PMMA layer itself has an adverse effect on the photovoltaic performance of the device.

To quantify the photocurrent improvement originating from the LDS action of the CQDs, the optical reflectance of the CISe devices is analyzed. Fig. 9d shows the measured reflectance spectra of the bare CISe solar cell and the T-richCD3/PMMA-coated device. These results indicate that the reflectance of the T-richCD3/PMMA coated CISe device slightly decreased within the 450–1100 nm wavelength range as compared to the bare CISe device. From the difference in the reflectance spectra, the photocurrent enhancement due to the AR effect could be estimated within the wavelength range of 300–1100 nm as follows:

where q is the elementary charge, Δ
R is the difference in the reflectance of the CISe devices with and without T-richCD3/PMMA, and ΦAM1.5G is the photon flux of the standard AM1.5G solar spectrum. The photocurrent gain from the AR origin is calculated to be 0.3 mA/cm2. Therefore, it can be inferred that the jSC gain may account for ~ 1.5 mA/cm2 of the overall photocurrent enhancement of 1.8 mA/cm2 (Fig. 9a), attributed to the down-converting performance of the CQDs. Notably, taking the adverse effect of the PMMA matrix (Fig. 9c) into account, the LDS effect of the CQDs reported herein could be higher than the above estimation and the performance of the device is compared with literature in Table S6.

Highly fluorescent and completely water-soluble CQDs were successfully synthesized within 5 min through microwave-assisted synthesis using EDTA and TTDDA monomers as the precursor. The functionalities and chemical structures of the molecular surface of CQDs were easily tuned by varying the initial feed ratio of the polyamidation monomers. The amide, carboxyl, etc., functional groups decorated on the surface of CQDs promoted good sensitivity and selectivity towards Cu2+ ions, and the sensitivity increased with increasing the TTDDA monomer in the precursor composition. The prepared CQDs exhibited low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility to MDCK and MDA-MB-231 cells. The in-vivo toxicity studies show that E-richCD1 and T-richCD1 induce no abnormalities in the development of zebrafish. Further, the high PLQY (53.3%) of T-richCD3 enabled to use them as LDS layer to enhance the CISe solar cell performance. These results demonstrated that CQDs synthesized from appropriate TTDDA and EDTA composition are suitable for applications including optoelectronics, metal ion sensor, drug delivery, and solar cell.
